name: Queen's Gambit
description: A classical opening with d4 and c4. One of the most important openings in chess.

moves.commentary: The queen's pawn stakes a claim in the center.

children.commentary: Black mirrors with d5, establishing a classical pawn center.
The Queen's Gambit - offering a pawn to gain central control.
The Queen's Gambit Declined. Black supports the center without taking the pawn.
Nc3 develops and increases pressure on d5.
Nf6 develops the knight to its natural square.
Bg5 pins the knight and increases pressure.
Be7 breaks the pin and completes development.
e3 solidifies the center. The mainline Queen's Gambit Declined.
The Queen's Gambit Accepted. Black takes the pawn but gives up the center.
The Slav Defense. Black supports d5 with the c-pawn.

defenses.name: Queen's Gambit Declined
defenses.profile: The QGD is the most classical response to the Queen's Gambit.

defenses.name: Slav Defense
defenses.profile: The Slav Defense maintains the pawn on d5.

strategy.keyIdea: Offer a pawn on c4 to gain central control with d4, then use space advantage to restrain Black's piece activity.
strategy.middleGamePlan: After the opening, focus on piece activity through the c-file and the b1-h7 diagonal. The minority attack (...a4-a5-b4-b5 for White with pawns on c4/d4) is a common long-term plan. In the Exchange Variation, look to dominate the open c-file with rooks.
strategy.watchOut: Accepting the gambit pawn and trying to hold it — it's usually better to return the pawn for development than to defend it under pressure.
typicalGoals: Control the center with d4 and c4
Develop the bishop to f4 or g5 before closing the center
Use the c-file for rook activity in the middlegame
Restrain Black's queenside counterplay with ...c5 or ...e5

quiz.question: Black accepts the Queen's Gambit and plays ...dxc4. What's your best approach?

options.text: Immediately win the pawn back with e3 and Bxc4
options.explanation: While this works, fixating on recapturing immediately can allow Black to equalize easily. Development and center control are the priority.

options.text: Play e3 and Bxc4 after developing — reclaim the pawn with a tempo
options.explanation: Correct. Developing first and then recapturing with the bishop gains a tempo by threatening Black's position while completing development.

options.text: Ignore the pawn and develop pieces as quickly as possible
options.explanation: In the QGD accepted, recapturing the c4 pawn after development is correct — you want both development AND central control.

default.opponentMoveSummary: Your opponent stepped off the main theory line with an unusual move, choosing their own path rather than the expected response.
default.whyBookBreaks: The standard line assumes a particular pawn or piece configuration that this move bypasses.
default.opponentStrategy: Your opponent is trying to reach a less-charted position where their preparation or intuition gives them an edge.
forwardGuidance: Prioritize completing your development — get all minor pieces off the back rank.
Control the center with pawns or piece pressure before launching any flank operations.

c6.opponentMoveSummary: Black played c6, defending d5 with the pawn and keeping the c8 bishop unblocked.
c6.whyBookBreaks: The Queen's Gambit main lines expect e6 (QGD) or dxc4 (QGA). c6 defends d5 without blocking the bishop.
c6.opponentStrategy: Slav Defense — Black keeps the bishop active and aims for a solid, flexible structure with counterplay options.
forwardGuidance: Develop your pieces actively; the Slav often leads to rich, strategic play.
Control the center with e3 and Nf3; be careful not to overextend.

Nf6.opponentMoveSummary: Black played Nf6, developing the knight and preparing a Grünfeld-style setup with g6.
Nf6.whyBookBreaks: The main lines expect e6 or dxc4. Nf6 allows White to build a center first, then Black will attack it.
Nf6.opponentStrategy: Grünfeld setup — Black allows White's center and then attacks it with pieces and pawn breaks.
forwardGuidance: Build your center with e4 when appropriate; Black will challenge it.
Develop solidly; the Grünfeld often leads to sharp, tactical positions.

e5.opponentMoveSummary: Black played e5, aggressively counterattacking in the center with a gambit.
e5.whyBookBreaks: The standard line expects e6 or dxc4. e5 is a sharp gambit that unbalances the position immediately.
e5.opponentStrategy: Albin Counter-Gambit — Black sacrifices a pawn to create immediate tactical chaos and unbalance the game.
forwardGuidance: Accept the center pawns carefully; Black gets compensation in development.
Complete your development before committing; the position can become very sharp.
